examples.title: Virtual Destructor Demonstration
examples.description: Shows why virtual destructors are needed for proper cleanup in polymorphic inheritance
examples.explanation: The virtual ~Base() ensures that when we delete through a base pointer, the derived class destructor is called first, then base destructor. This properly frees all allocated memory in both classes.

examples.title: Destructor Order in Inheritance
examples.description: Demonstrates the order of constructor and destructor calls in single inheritance
examples.explanation: Constructors run from Base to Derived (build-up). Destructors run from Derived to Base (tear-down). This reverse order ensures derived class dependencies are cleaned up before base class.

examples.title: RAII with Destructor
examples.description: Implements Resource Acquisition Is Initialization pattern using destructor for automatic resource management
examples.explanation: RAII pattern: File is opened in constructor and automatically closed in destructor. No manual cleanup needed. When the function ends (normally or due to exception), destructor runs and releases resource.

examples.title: Multiple Inheritance Destructor Order
examples.description: Shows destructor execution order in multiple inheritance scenarios
examples.explanation: In multiple inheritance, destructors execute in reverse order of base class declaration (C, then B, then A). When deleting through A*, only A's destructor runs (virtual ensures this) but B's part leaks unless C's destructor is called. Always use delete on the most derived type or ensure all base classes have virtual destructors.

examples.title: Pure Virtual Destructor
examples.description: Demonstrates pure virtual destructor making abstract class with virtual cleanup
examples.explanation: A pure virtual destructor must be defined (unlike pure virtual functions). It makes the class abstract while ensuring proper cleanup through polymorphic deletion. Both Concrete and AbstractBase destructors are called when deleting through base pointer.
